name: Osechi Ryori Jubilee Box Japan
desc: Traditional tiered boxes (jubako) containing various symbolic foods eaten during the first three days of the year in Japan.

name: Ozoni Mochi Soup Japan
desc: A savory miso or soy-based broth containing toasted rice cakes. A staple breakfast dish for New Year's Day in Japan.

name: Iwai-bashi Celebration Chopsticks Japan
desc: Special thick chopsticks tapered at both ends, used specifically for eating festive New Year meals in Japan.

name: Ebi Longevity Shrimp Japan
desc: Cooked shrimp represent a long life until your back bends like a prawn. A key component of festive platters in Japan.

name: Kuromame Black Soybeans Japan
desc: Sweet simmered black beans that symbolize a wish for health and the ability to work hard (mame) in Japan.

name: Kamaboko Red and White Fishcake Japan
desc: Sliced fishcakes in red and white, colors that represent celebration and the rising sun in the culture of Japan.

name: Daidai New Year Citrus Japan
desc: A bitter orange placed atop Kagami Mochi, symbolizing the continuity of generations within a family in Japan.

name: Datemaki Sweet Rolled Omelet Japan
desc: A sweet omelet mixed with fish paste, rolled to look like a scroll, symbolizing a wish for scholarship and knowledge in Japan.

name: Tazukuri Candied Sardines Japan
desc: Small dried sardines cooked in soy sauce and sugar, symbolizing a prayer for a bountiful harvest in Japan.

name: Kuri Kinton Chestnut Gold Japan
desc: Mashed sweet potatoes with chestnuts. Its golden color represents a wish for financial prosperity and wealth in Japan.

name: O-toso Spiced Medicinal Sake Japan
desc: Special medicinal sake drunk on New Year's morning to wash away the previous year's maladies and ensure health in Japan.

name: Toshikoshi Soba New Year Eve Noodles Japan
desc: Buckwheat noodles eaten on New Year's Eve. Their long shape represents a long life, while their easy-to-cut nature signifies cutting off the year's hardships in Japan.
